draft.introduction: This celebrated ghazal from Hafiz's Divan meditates on the transformative power of divine love and the futility of rational calculation in the face of spiritual ecstasy.

translation.text: I have learned that each morning the Beloved rises and scatters a new grace over the world. The people of reason have their reasons; the lovers have something else entirely. Give the sermon of the ascetic to the wind—bring wine. What good is talk of sin and repentance to those who have already lost themselves?

translator_notes.note: The opposition between the ascetic (zahid) and the lover (ashiq) is a recurring structural device in Hafiz. The ascetic represents conventional piety guided by fear; the lover represents the Sufi path of reckless devotion. Hafiz consistently sides with the lover, a stance that made him controversial among the orthodox.
